statement.markdown:
You are given a string representing an HTML list item. The string may contain one or more `
  • ` tags. Your task is to validate if the HTML list item is well-formed. A well-formed HTML list item has all its `
  • ` tags properly closed. For example, the string `
  • Item 1
  • Item 2
    • ` is well-formed, but the string `
  • Item 1` is not well-formed. You need to return `true` if the string is well-formed and `false` otherwise.
statement.inputFormat: A string containing one or more HTML list items.
statement.outputFormat: A boolean indicating whether the HTML list item is well-formed.
statement.notes: You can assume that the input string will not contain any other HTML tags besides `<li>`. You can also assume that the input string will not contain any duplicate `<li>` tags.

constraints.description: The input string containing one or more HTML list items.

examples.explanation: The string is well-formed because all <li> tags are properly closed.
The string is not well-formed because the <li> tag is not properly closed.

hints.text: You can use a stack to keep track of the opening <li> tags.

editorial.title: Fixing Broken HTML List Tags
editorial.intuition: To solve this problem, we can use a stack to keep track of the opening <li> tags. We push each opening <li> tag onto the stack and pop the stack when we encounter a closing <li> tag. If we encounter an opening <li> tag when the stack is empty, it means the previous <li> tag was not properly closed, so we return false.
editorial.bruteForce: We can use the following approach to solve this problem. We can iterate over the string and check if each substring is a well-formed HTML list item. If we find a substring that is not a well-formed HTML list item, we return false. If we finish iterating over the string and do not find any substrings that are not well-formed HTML list items, we return true.
editorial.betterSolution: We can use a stack to keep track of the opening <li> tags. We push each opening <li> tag onto the stack and pop the stack when we encounter a closing <li> tag. If we encounter an opening <li> tag when the stack is empty, it means the previous <li> tag was not properly closed, so we return false.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution uses a stack to keep track of the opening <li> tags. We push each opening <li> tag onto the stack and pop the stack when we encounter a closing <li> tag. We iterate over the string and check if each substring is a well-formed HTML list item. If we find a substring that is not a well-formed HTML list item, we return false. If we finish iterating over the string and do not find any substrings that are not well-formed HTML list items, we return true.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness is as follows. We use a stack to keep track of the opening <li> tags. We push each opening <li> tag onto the stack and pop the stack when we encounter a closing <li> tag. If we encounter an opening <li> tag when the stack is empty, it means the previous <li> tag was not properly closed, so we return false. If we finish iterating over the string and do not find any substrings that are not well-formed HTML list items, we return true.

Not popping the stack when encountering a closing <li> tag
